titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the American Red Cross Vassar Relief Unit Collection, 1910-1961 (bulk 1916-1926)
titlestmt.author: Inventory prepared by Archives and Special Collections Library, Vassar College Libraries

publicationstmt.publisher: Archives and Special Collections Library, Vassar College Libraries

profiledesc.creation: Finding aid encoded by Laura Streett, 2008.

did.head: Collection Summary
did.unittitle: American Red Cross Vassar Relief Unit Records

repository.label: Repository:
repository.corpname: Archives and Special Collections Library, Vassar College Libraries

did.abstract: Records concerning the American Red Cross Vassar Relief Unit's work in France during and after World War I, including letters, photographs, reports, financial statements, account books, artwork, clippings, Red Cross publications, Vassar Unit newsletters, and realia.

bioghist.head: Administrative History
bioghist.p: In January 1918, the Associate Alumnae of Vassar College voted to send a delegation of Vassar graduates to France to assist in the relief efforts of the American Red Cross. The Vassar Unit left the Red Cross recreation huts in Savenay at the end of February, 1919 and began their relief work in Verdun in mid-March after regrouping in Paris. The Unit's nurse, Gertrude Hodgman, had already agreed to a position with the Red Cross's Palestine Commission, so Margaret Tupper was recruited to join the Unit in Verdun as their new nurse. Ruth Crippen and Lida Little also returned to the United States in early 1919.

In Verdun, the relief unit worked with the local government and the French Red Cross to provide services and supplies to French refugees returning to Verdun after the end of the war. The unit in Verdun set up a dispensary and a milk station, known as the "Goutte de Lait," or "Drop of Milk," which pasteurized and distributed free milk to the children of Verdun. The work in Verdun was transition from the original members of the unit to Elsa Butler Grove in January of 1919, and eventually entirely into the management of the French by September of 1920.

list.item: Mabel Kirk (VC 1900) - Chairman, Alumnae Endowment Committee
Fanny P. Townsend (VC 1902) - Chairman, General War Service Committee of the Alumnae

head.emph: Members of the Vassar Red Cross Relief Unit

list.item: Lida A. Little (VC 1906) - Secretary, 1918 Aug - 1919 Apr
Elsa Butler Grove (VC 1905) - Vice-President / Medical social worker, 1919 Dec - 1920 Sep
Ruth Crippen (VC 1904) - Social worker, 1918 Aug - 1919 Mar(?)
Ruth Cutler (VC 1912) - Social worker, 1918 Aug - 1918 Dec 27 (Died from pneumonia in Paris)
Gertrude Hodgman (VC 1912) - Head Nurse, 1918 Aug - 1919 Feb(?)
Ethel M. Riddle (VC 1910) - Head Social Worker, 1918 Aug - 1919 Feb(?)
Elinor Prudden (VC 1912) - Social worker, 1918 Aug - After 1920 Feb(?)
Margaret Tupper (VC 1906) - Nurse, 1919 Apr - Before 1920 Apr(?)

head.emph: Members of the Vassar Y.M.C.A. Canteen Unit

list.item: Elizabeth K. Van der Veer (VC 1906) - Unit head
Dorothea Gay (VC 1911), 1918 Aug - 1918 Nov 9 (Died from pneumonia in Bordeaux)
Gertrude Valentine (VC 1912), 1918 Sep - 1919 Jun (Died in an automobile accident in Le Mans)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Content Note
scopecontent.p: Records concerning the American Red Cross Vassar Relief Unit's work in France during and after World War I, including letters, photographs, reports, financial statements, account books, artwork, clippings, Red Cross publications, Vassar Unit newsletters, and realia.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The collection is organized into eleven series.

accessrestrict.head: Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research according to the regulations of the Vassar College Archives and Special Collections Library without any additional restrictions.

userestrict.head: Restrictions on Use
userestrict.p: Permission to quote (publish) from unpublished or previously published material must be obtained as described in the regulations of the Vassar College Archives and Special Collections Library.

p.extref: Letters written by Fanny P. Simpson Townsend during her time as a Vassar student were collected and published as "The Turn of the Century Group" by her daughter, Frances Townsend Marshall, in 1973.[Main Library LD7186 .S55 1973]

p.archref: Vassar College Training Camp for Nurses Records

p.extref: Lambie, Margaret. Verdun Experiences. Washington: Courant Press, 1945. [Main Library D570.9 .L28 1945]

p.archref: The Vassar College Subject Files [folders 26.57-26.60] contain additional information on the Vassar Unit.

separatedmaterial.head: Separated Materials
separatedmaterial.p: A small collection of books primarily belonging to Margaret Lambie was removed from the collection and catalogued separately.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: The majority of materials in the Vassar Unit Collection were gifted to the Vassar Library in 1956 by Elsa Butler Grove and in 1960 by Margaret Lambie. These materials were partially processed at an unknown date. Materials were added to and separated from the collection at unknown dates. Photographs were separated from the collection and added to the Vassar College Photograph Files, and then returned to the Vassar Unit Collection. Materials were also separated from the Elsa Butler Grove Papers and added to the Vassar Unit Collection.

The collection was re-processed in 2025 by Emma Gronbeck, with the goal of re-grouping material in the collection by provenance. As a result, many materials created by Elsa Butler Grove were separated from the collection and added to the Elsa Butler Grove Papers, including correspondence, photographs, reports, and realia.

Materials in the collection were grouped together by creator when one could be identified. Materials without a discernible provenance were added to either Series I. General or Series X. Publications. Because letters from both sides of many correspondences were present in the collection, the decision was made to place letters written by an individual with a dedicated series in the collection in that series (i.e. letters written by Fanny P. Townsend are almost all collected in Series VII. Fanny P. Townsend Papers), so in many instances a researcher will need folders from two different series in order to see both sides of a conversation.

acqinfo.head: Acquisition Information
acqinfo.p: Gift of Lida A. Little (VC 1906), 1 Jul 1925.
Gift of Elsa Butler Grove (VC 1905), Jun 1956.
Some materials of Marion R. Meyers (VC 1910) were the gift of Paula R. Hyatt (niece of Marion Meyers), 4 Mar 1988.
Ruth Cutler's papers were the gift of Gift of Pamela Fricke, February 2009.
The illuminated list of alumnae abroad created by Margaret Beck (VC 1919) in Series I was transferred from the Alumnae House in 2018. (A2018-014)

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: American Red Cross Vassar Relief Unit Collection, Archives and Special Collections Library, Vassar College Libraries.

did.unittitle: Series I. General,

scopecontent.head: Scope and Content Note
scopecontent.p: This series contains general and miscellaneous records related to the Vassar Red Cross Relief Unit, Y.M.C.A. Canteen Unit, Vassar Unit Committee, and activities of the Alumnae Association of Vassar College related to alumnae engaged in war work and relief services abroad. Correspondence includes letters to and from Helen Kenyon, the President of the Alumnae Association, Harietta Sawyer, the Executive Secretary; and Mabel Horst Kirk, Chairman of the Alumnae Endowment Committee, as well as between other related persons and organizations. The "Vassar Alumnae in Foreign Service" subseries consists mainly of letters sent to the Alumnae Association by alumnae abroad or their close relatives, generally describing the work they were engaged in overseas. Other materials included in this series are photographs of the Vassar Unit and its members, printed ephemera, financial and operational records of the Vassar Unit, and writings by members of the Vassar Unit on their experiences.

arrangement.p: Organized into 6 subseries: Correspondence, Photographs, Printed Ephemera, Vassar Alumnae in Foreign Service, Vassar Unit and AAVC Records, and Writings.

did.unittitle: Series II. Ruth Crippen Papers,

bioghist.head: Biographical Note
bioghist.p: Ruth Hicks Crippen (1881-1972) was born in 1881 to J. Hicks and Alice Hotchkiss Crippen and graduated from Vassar College in 1905. She taught English at West High School in Rochester, New York, for most of her career, until her retirement in 1951.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Content Note
scopecontent.p: This series includes letters from Ruth Crippen (VC 1905) to her family and to Fanny Townsend, as well as one letter from Vassar College President James Monroe Taylor to Crippen regarding the main gate to the college.

did.unittitle: Series III. Ruth Cutler Papers,

bioghist.head: Biographical Note
bioghist.p: Ruth Cutler was a member of the Vassar College Class of 1912. She was an active supporter of the Goodfellowship Club while on campus, a social club for Vassar's maintenance staff. In 1918, she traveled to France to work as part of the American Red Cross Vassar Relief Unit. Only four weeks later she became ill with influenza and died.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Content Note
scopecontent.p: This series includes letters from Ruth Cutler (VC 1912) to her family, and four letters from Elinor Prudden (also VC 1912), who traveled with Cutler. The letters document Cutler's travel by ship to France and her service there with the Red Cross. Prudden’s letters are to Cutler’s family describing the circumstances of Cutler’s death from pneumonia.

did.unittitle: Series IV. Gertrude Hodgman Papers,

bioghist.head: Biographical Note
bioghist.p: Gertrude Hodgman graduated from Vassar College in 1912 and earned her nursing diploma from Johns Hopkins in 1916. She was awarded the medal of Verdun's Société Blessaire aux Militaire for her service after the war, and went on to continue her service with the Red Cross in Syria from 1919 to 1922. After returning to the United States she earned her master's from Columbia University and taught nursing at Yale for several years between various international jobs.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Content Note
scopecontent.p: This series contains correspondence to and from Gertrude Hodgman, who served as the nurse of the Vassar Unit through December of 1918. Also included are several documents related to the American Red Cross Commission to Palestine, clippings about Hodgman, and a timeline of Hodgman's life.

did.unittitle: Series V. Margaret Lambie Papers,

bioghist.head: Biographical Note
bioghist.p: Margaret Lambie (1885-1982) was born in Northampton, Massachusetts to Jasper E. Lambie and Henrietta E. Bryan. She graduated from Vassar College in 1907 and was an active suffragist. In 1918, she was appointed director of the Vassar Red Cross Relief Unit and worked in Savenay and Verdun. She earned her law degree from George Washington University Law School in 1925 and a certificate in international law in 1926 from the Hague Academy of International Law in the Netherlands. In 1928, she was admitted to practice law in New York and the District of Columbia, and relocated to Washington D.C., where she began a long career in international law. She retired from law practice and relocated to Essex, Massachusetts in 1965, and passed away in 1982 at the age of 97.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Content Note
scopecontent.p: This series contains materials collected and created by Vassar Unit Director, Margaret Lambie. It includes correspondence between Margaret Lambie and her family, Vassar Unit members, and French colleagues and acquaintances; clippings and printed ephemera related to Lambie, the Vassar Unit, and Verdun; reports and other records of the Vassar Unit; photographs of the "Goutte de Lait," Lambie, and others; and materials related to Lambie's article, "Verdun Experiences," which was republished as a book after the Second World War. Also included are copies of the pamphlet, "To You, the Girls of France," which was created by Vassar students to be distributed to French girls' and women's schools to show support and solidarity through writing and poetry. This pamphlet was one of many created by American colleges and universities and taken to France by John H. Finley. Many schools created handmade responses to this pamphlet in order to show their appreciation, featuring prose, poetry, illustrations, and paintings. These response pamphlets were collected by Finley and given to the Alumnae Association of Vassar College.

did.unittitle: Series VI. Lida A. Little Papers,

bioghist.head: Biographical Note
bioghist.p: Lida Agnese Little (1883-1964) was born in Crocker, Iowa on January 18, 1883 to Gust B. Little (1858-1901) and Ora M. (Armstrong) Little (1858-1890). When Little was orphaned at 18 after the death of her father, she was taken in by her widowed paternal aunt, Luella M. (Little) Wilson, who would occupy a parental role for Little for the rest of her life. With family, Little often went by the nickname "Dolly." Jarvis Douglas Little (1899-1956) was Little's cousin, the son of her father's younger brother, William Douglas Little. (1862-1901).

Little graduated from Vassar College as part of the Class of 1906 and after graduation, taught English, Latin, and History at the Stevan School of Girls in Chicago Illinois. In 1918, she joined the Vassar Red Cross Relief Unit as its secretary and spent time in Savenay, France. After the war, she continued to work for the Alumnae Association for several years, before returning home to California in 1930 and working as a social worker for seniors in Los Angeles. In 1939, she was hospitalized in Camarillo State Mental Hospital and remained in-patient there for many years. She was transferred to Patton State Hospital in the mid-1950s and passed away there in 1964.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Content Note
scopecontent.p: This series contains materials collected and created by Vassar Unit Secretary, Lida A. Little. It includes correspondence between Little and her family (primarily her aunt, Luella M. Wilson), Vassar Unit members, and French colleagues and acquaintances; clippings and printed ephemera related the Vassar Unit and Verdun; an account book, reports, and other records of the Vassar Unit; medical and travel documents; photographs of Little, France, and the Vassar Unit; and writings by Little, including two works of fiction.

did.unittitle: Series VII. Marion R. Meyers Papers,

bioghist.head: Biographical Note
bioghist.p: Marion Rose Meyers (1888-1974) was born in Rochester, New York, and graduated from Vassar College in 1910. After graduation, she lived and studied in France, Italy, and Switzerland. In 1918, she joined the Vassar Red Cross Relief Unit as the unit's dietician. She was honored by the City of Verdun for her work with the Vassar Unit's milk station, the "Goutte de Lait." After returning to the United States, Meyers became a visiting social worker in the Rochester Public School District for over twenty years. She received her Master's in Child Development from the University of Rochester in 1933. She retired from social work in 1948 and relocated to Coronado, California. She passed away in 1974, at the age of 86.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Content Note
scopecontent.p: This series contains materials collected and created by Marion R. Meyers, the Vassar Unit's dietician. It includes correspondence from Meyers to her family, Fanny P. Townsend, and others; Red Cross and travel documents; photographs; and records of the Vassar Unit and "Goutte de Lait."

did.unittitle: Series VIII. Fanny P. Townsend Papers,

bioghist.head: Biographical Note
bioghist.p: Fanny P. Simpson (1881-1970) graduated from Vassar College in 1902. She married Edward P. Townsend, a banker, in 1905 and had one daughter, Frances (Townsend) Marshall (VC 1930) in 1908. Townsend was Chairman of the General War Service Committee of the Alumnae Association of Vassar College during World War I and remained active in the Alumnae Association for many years. She also worked with other women's organizations throughout her life, including the Y.W.C.A., Women's University Club, and the Women's City Club of New York.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Content Note
scopecontent.p: This series contains materials collected and created by Fanny P. (Simpson) Townsend, Chairman of the General War Service Committee of the Alumnae Association of Vassar College. It primarily includes correspondence between Townsend and various members of the Vassar Unit, Canteen Unit, and Alumnae Association, as well as other Vassar alumnae abroad. It also includes some clippings, a photograph album, printed ephemera, reports received from Elsa Butler Grove, and an article about the Vassar Unit published by Townsend in the Vassar Quarterly.

did.unittitle: Series IX. Gertrude C. Valentine Papers,

bioghist.head: Biographical Note
bioghist.p: Gertrude C. Valentine graduated from Vassar College in 1912. She was a member of the Y.M.C.A. Canteen Unit and died in an automobile accident in Le Mans, France, in June, 1919.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Content Note
scopecontent.p: This series contains letters and reports from Gertrude C. Valentine, a member of the Y.M.C.A. Canteen Unit to her family and other members of the Vassar Unit. Also included are photographs of Valentine and of her funeral in 1919.

did.unittitle: Series X. Elizabeth K. Van der Veer Papers,

bioghist.head: Biographical Note
bioghist.p: Elizabeth K. Van der Veer (1884-1963) was born on April 20, 1884 in Trenton, New Jersey, to Augustus and Evelyn Gaston Van der Veer. She graduated from Vassar College as part of the class of 1906 and went on to have a life-long career in social work. After graduating from Vassar, she taught English and French at Sommerville High School in New Jersey for ten years. When the Alumnae Association of Vassar College was organizing volunteers for work overseas, she was selected to direct the Vassar Y.M.C.A. Canteen Unit, which was stationed in Bordeaux, France, through the end of World War I. Upon returning to the United States, Van der Veer received training in social work and spent several years working as a social worker and investigator for Somerset Hospital. In 1932, she was appointed director of the Somerset County Welfare Board, a position she retained for 22 years until her retirement in 1954. She passed away in 1963 after a short illness.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Content Note
scopecontent.p: This series contains correspondence to and from Elizabeth K. Van der Veer, who headed the Vassar Y.M.C.A. Canteen Unit.

did.unittitle: Series XI. Publications,

scopecontent.head: Scope and Content Note
scopecontent.p: This series contains clippings and publications related to the Vassar Unit, Vassar alumnae in work overseas, and Verdun. The newsletter "Toot Sweet" was published by the Vassar Unit members stationed in Savenay, France in 1918, and later Verdun from 1919-1921.

did.unittitle: Series XII. Realia,

scopecontent.head: Scope and Content Note
scopecontent.p: This series contains objects owned or collected by members of the Vassar Unit, including a Red Cross uniform, medals and pins, other Red Cross accessories, two sets of "Nénette and Rintintin" yarn charms, a large piece of shrapnel, machine gun cartridges, and other miscellaneous objects.

arrangement.p: Organized into six subseries by object provenance: General, Margaret Lambie, Lida A. Little, Fanny P. Townsend, Margaret Tupper, and Gertrude C. Valentine.
